reason: Courtly plotting, marriages and betrayals as Richard schemes in the shadows for England’s crown.
The Madness of King George shows ministers, royals and dukes jockeying for power during King George III’s crisis.
Mr. Smith Goes to Washington captures Senate lobbying, backroom deals and a lone filibuster against political machines.
Henry V portrays court conspiracies and political pressure in the lead-up to Agincourt, far from the battlefield.
Paths of Glory depicts military tribunal politics and chain-of-command betrayals in a ruthless command structure.
Amadeus peels back Habsburg court rivalries as Mozart and Salieri vie for imperial favor in whispered intrigues.
Bridge on the River Kwai spotlights command politics and willful betrayals among POW officers far from home.
Patton delves into a general’s political games and rivalries within the Allied high command during WWII.
Dr. Strangelove satirizes Cold War brinkmanship with generals and politicians plotting nuclear gambits in secret.
Jefferson in Paris reveals diplomatic maneuvering, salons and clandestine alliances on the eve of revolution.
